steps.title: Question type
steps.content: Standard English conventions — verb form. The subject "the International Astronomical Union" needs a finite main verb. There is no other conjugated verb elsewhere in the sentence, so the blank must supply one.

steps.title: Reasoning
steps.content: "Would finally acknowledge" is a finite verb phrase (past-tense narrative "would," meaning "eventually did"). With it, the sentence has a complete main clause, and "naming a crater on Venus after her" works as a trailing participial phrase modifying the action.

steps.title: Per-choice elimination
steps.content:
• Choice A — Union would finally acknowledge: supplies a finite main verb, so the sentence is grammatically complete. ✓
  • Choice B — Union to finally acknowledge: an infinitive is non-finite, so the sentence has no main verb — sentence fragment. ✗
  • Choice C — Union, having finally acknowledged: a perfect participial phrase is non-finite and only modifies the subject, leaving the sentence without a main verb — fragment. ✗
  • Choice D — Union, finally acknowledging: a present participial phrase is non-finite, so again no main verb — fragment. ✗
A is correct.
